catalyst.source: The PtnanoparticleTiO 2 nanotube composite is shown to greatly promote the photocatalytic conversion of carbon dioxide and water vapor into methane, a behavior attributed to the homogeneous distribution of metal co-catalyst nanoparticles over the TiO 2 nanotube array surface providing a large number of active reduction sites.
catalyst.context: Using a rapid microwave-assisted solvothermal approach ultrafine Pt nanoparticles are synthesized and deposited in situ within high aspect ratio nanotube arrays.Adjusting the initial concentration of metal ion precursor inside the nanotube support controls the resulting Pt nanoparticle sizes.The PtnanoparticleTiO 2 nanotube composite is shown to greatly promote the photocatalytic conversion of carbon dioxide and water vapor into methane, a behavior attributed to the homogeneous distribution of metal co-catalyst nanoparticles over the TiO 2 nanotube array surface providing a large number of active reduction sites.The novelty and flexibility of the technique, described herein, could prove useful for the deposition of metal, metal alloy, or metal oxide nanoparticles within a variety of nanotubular or nanoporous material systems with the resulting nanocomposites useful in catalysis, photocatalysis, photovoltaic, and photoelectrochemical applications.

catalyst.source: TiO 2 nanotube arrays were prepared by anodic oxidation of titanium foil in an electrolyte consisting of 0.3 (wt) ammonium fluoride in 3 vol water containing ethylene glycol electrolyte at 55 V.
catalyst.context: TiO 2 nanotube arrays were prepared by anodic oxidation of titanium foil in an electrolyte consisting of 0.3 (wt) ammonium fluoride in 3 vol water containing ethylene glycol electrolyte at 55 V. 20 Nanotube array samples of 30 mm in length were prepared by 7 h anodization.The average inner pore diameter of the nanotube arrays, estimated from FE-SEM images, was about 70 nm with a wall thickness of about 20 nm.Debris formed during the anodization process was removed by ultrasonic agitation.The as-prepared amorphous samples were crystallized by annealing at 540 C for 3 h in an oxygen atmosphere.Pt nanoparticle loading was performed via a microwave assisted solvothermal method by using a MARS-5 microwave digestion system operated at a frequency of 2.45 GHz with a power of 300 W. PtTiO 2 composites or bare TiO 2 nanotubes of 2 cm A 1 cm size were loaded into a stainless steel reaction chamber having a quartz window through which the sample can be exposed to light.After a sample was loaded into the reaction chamber, the 7.56 cm 3 chamber was evacuated to 10 A3 Torr, after which carbon dioxide (99.99), ultra-high purity Helium (how many 99.999), or Argon (99.99) with a flow rate of 500 sccm (standard cubic centimetres per minute) were passed though a bubbler containing deionized water before entering the reaction chamber.After the chamber was flushed for 10 min both inlet and outlet valves were closed maintaining a nominal excess pressure of less than 1.0 psi.Under test, the samples within the reaction chambers were exposed to AM 1.5G sunlight (100 mW cm A2 ) for one hour; the equilibrium temperature of the chamber under test was about 43 AE 3 C. Reaction products were analyzed using a Shimadzu gas chromatograph (GC-17A) equipped with flame ionization and thermal conductivity detectors.

catalyst.source: PtTiO 2 composites or bare TiO 2 nanotubes of 2 cm A 1 cm size were loaded into a stainless steel reaction chamber having a quartz window through which the sample can be exposed to light.
catalyst.context: TiO 2 nanotube arrays were prepared by anodic oxidation of titanium foil in an electrolyte consisting of 0.3 (wt) ammonium fluoride in 3 vol water containing ethylene glycol electrolyte at 55 V. 20 Nanotube array samples of 30 mm in length were prepared by 7 h anodization.The average inner pore diameter of the nanotube arrays, estimated from FE-SEM images, was about 70 nm with a wall thickness of about 20 nm.Debris formed during the anodization process was removed by ultrasonic agitation.The as-prepared amorphous samples were crystallized by annealing at 540 C for 3 h in an oxygen atmosphere.Pt nanoparticle loading was performed via a microwave assisted solvothermal method by using a MARS-5 microwave digestion system operated at a frequency of 2.45 GHz with a power of 300 W. PtTiO 2 composites or bare TiO 2 nanotubes of 2 cm A 1 cm size were loaded into a stainless steel reaction chamber having a quartz window through which the sample can be exposed to light.After a sample was loaded into the reaction chamber, the 7.56 cm 3 chamber was evacuated to 10 A3 Torr, after which carbon dioxide (99.99), ultra-high purity Helium (how many 99.999), or Argon (99.99) with a flow rate of 500 sccm (standard cubic centimetres per minute) were passed though a bubbler containing deionized water before entering the reaction chamber.After the chamber was flushed for 10 min both inlet and outlet valves were closed maintaining a nominal excess pressure of less than 1.0 psi.Under test, the samples within the reaction chambers were exposed to AM 1.5G sunlight (100 mW cm A2 ) for one hour; the equilibrium temperature of the chamber under test was about 43 AE 3 C. Reaction products were analyzed using a Shimadzu gas chromatograph (GC-17A) equipped with flame ionization and thermal conductivity detectors.

catalyst.source: In summary, for the first time a uniform dispersion of ultrafine Pt nanoparticles along the length of a long, i.e. several tens of microns, TiO 2 nanotube support is realized via a rapid microwave-assisted solvothermal approach.
catalyst.context: In summary, for the first time a uniform dispersion of ultrafine Pt nanoparticles along the length of a long, i.e. several tens of microns, TiO 2 nanotube support is realized via a rapid microwave-assisted solvothermal approach.Nanoparticle size is controlled by adjusting the concentration of the metal ion precursor.The described technique should prove effective for deposition upon a variety of semiconductor nanoporous or nanotube compositions, with different metal, metal alloy or metal oxide nanoparticles, the resulting materials of which should have great use in catalysis and photoelectric conversion applications.Pt is known to be more effective at reducing water for hydrogen generation, whereas Cu is likely to be more active in reducing CO 2 .Hence, an appropriate dispersion (side by side) of Cu and Pt co-catalysts on the nanotube array surface is expected to carry out the partial reactions and subsequent hydrocarbon formation at much higher rates. Fig. 1 (a) Depiction of in situ deposition of ultra-fine Pt nanoparticles inside the nanotubes.(b) FESEM image of self-organized TiO 2 nanotube arrays, perpendicularly oriented from the substrate, with a length of z 30 mm.The insert is a FESEM image at a higher magnification.(c) Top view image of Pt loaded TiO 2 nanotube arrays.(d) XRD patterns of TiO 2 nanotube and Pt-nanoparticleTiO 2 nanotube composite. Fig. 2 (a) Bright field TEM image and (b) dark field STEM image of Pt nanoparticle loaded (Pt 4 0.15 mM) TiO 2 nanotube arrays (c) bright field TEM image of the region circled in (a), and (d) size histogram of Pt nanoparticles determined from TEM images. Fig. 3 (a) A typical TEM image of Pt nanoparticle loaded TiO 2 nanotube arrays (Pt 4 0.3 mM); (b) size histogram of Pt nanoparticles analyzed from TEM images. Fig. 4 (a) A typical TEM image of Sample 1, when the Pt 4 ion precursor concentration is 0.075 mM; (b) histogram of the diameter of Pt nanoparticles determined from TEM measurement. Fig. 5 Methane production rates from bare TiO 2 nanotube and Pt-TiO 2 nanotube composite (Sample 2, Pt 4 0.15 mM) in carbon dioxidewater vapor and argonwater vapor environments. Published on 01 August 2011.Downloaded by University of Illinois -Urbana on 16062013 04:39:14.View Article Online Partial support of this work through the Department of Energy, Grant No. DE-SC0001087, is gratefully acknowledged.

light source.context: TiO 2 nanotube arrays were prepared by anodic oxidation of titanium foil in an electrolyte consisting of 0.3 (wt) ammonium fluoride in 3 vol water containing ethylene glycol electrolyte at 55 V. 20 Nanotube array samples of 30 mm in length were prepared by 7 h anodization.The average inner pore diameter of the nanotube arrays, estimated from FE-SEM images, was about 70 nm with a wall thickness of about 20 nm.Debris formed during the anodization process was removed by ultrasonic agitation.The as-prepared amorphous samples were crystallized by annealing at 540 C for 3 h in an oxygen atmosphere.Pt nanoparticle loading was performed via a microwave assisted solvothermal method by using a MARS-5 microwave digestion system operated at a frequency of 2.45 GHz with a power of 300 W. PtTiO 2 composites or bare TiO 2 nanotubes of 2 cm A 1 cm size were loaded into a stainless steel reaction chamber having a quartz window through which the sample can be exposed to light.After a sample was loaded into the reaction chamber, the 7.56 cm 3 chamber was evacuated to 10 A3 Torr, after which carbon dioxide (99.99), ultra-high purity Helium (how many 99.999), or Argon (99.99) with a flow rate of 500 sccm (standard cubic centimetres per minute) were passed though a bubbler containing deionized water before entering the reaction chamber.After the chamber was flushed for 10 min both inlet and outlet valves were closed maintaining a nominal excess pressure of less than 1.0 psi.Under test, the samples within the reaction chambers were exposed to AM 1.5G sunlight (100 mW cm A2 ) for one hour; the equilibrium temperature of the chamber under test was about 43 AE 3 C. Reaction products were analyzed using a Shimadzu gas chromatograph (GC-17A) equipped with flame ionization and thermal conductivity detectors.
